name: Predictive health deterioration & early warning
description: AI analyses resident data to predict falls/deterioration. Aston Uni building UK's first smart care home.
current_process: Care staff monitor residents through scheduled observational rounds (typically every 1–2 hours overnight, more frequently during the day), recording vital signs, mobility observations, food and fluid intake, and behavioural changes in paper or digital daily logs. Falls risk is assessed using manual tools such as the Morse Fall Scale or STRATIFY at admission and periodically thereafter. Deterioration is detected reactively — staff notice changes in condition during routine interactions and escalate concerns verbally to senior carers or nurses. Early warning scores (e.g., NEWS2) may be used in nursing homes but are often recorded manually and checked infrequently. Patterns across multiple data points (e.g., declining fluid intake + reduced mobility + mood change) are rarely connected systematically, meaning deterioration is often identified only after a fall or acute episode has already occurred.
time_spent: Observational rounds and documentation consume an estimated 2–4 hours of collective staff time per shift across a typical care home. Manual falls risk assessments take 15–30 minutes per resident and are typically reviewed quarterly or after an incident. The reactive nature of current monitoring means that early warning signs are often missed, leading to emergency hospital admissions — falls alone result in approximately 2,000 avoidable hospital admissions per day across the UK according to NHS England data.
ai_solution: AI analyses continuous streams of resident data — vital signs, mobility patterns, food/fluid intake, sleep quality, medication changes, and behavioural observations — to predict health deterioration and falls before they occur. Specific capabilities include: (1) Falls prediction — Cera's AI tool predicts fall risk with up to 97% accuracy and is now deployed across more than two-thirds of NHS care systems, covering 2 million+ home care visits per month. It flags up to 5,000 high-risk alerts daily. (2) Deterioration detection — monitoring for early signs of infections (UTIs, chest infections), dehydration, and cognitive decline by identifying subtle pattern changes across multiple data sources. (3) Virus outbreak prediction — detecting symptoms of COVID-19, flu, RSV, and norovirus to enable early intervention. (4) Smart environment monitoring — Aston University's Smart Care Home project (funded by Innovate UK, partnered with Lee Mount Healthcare) is developing an AI-powered system that combines health metrics, care needs, and personal preferences for real-time decision support and personalised care plans. (5) Automated alerting — high-risk predictions trigger immediate notifications to care staff and clinical leads.
buyer_language: "We only find out something's wrong after a resident has already fallen or been rushed to hospital. By then it's too late. I wish we had a way to spot the warning signs earlier — before things get serious — without needing a nurse watching every resident around the clock."
outcome_statement: Falls and health deterioration predicted days in advance with up to 97% accuracy — enabling early intervention that reduces emergency hospital admissions by up to 70% and keeps residents safer in the home.
regulatory_alignment: Maps to CQC Quality Statements under Safe (preventing avoidable harm, falls prevention, risk management), Effective (monitoring health outcomes, responding to deterioration), and Responsive (person-centred, proactive care). Aligns with the Digitising Social Care programme's promotion of innovative care technologies, including acoustic monitoring and AI for falls reduction. The 10 Year Health Plan includes an AI-powered early warning system for real-time safety monitoring. The government estimates falls-reduction technology can save the NHS over £2 billion annually.
existing_tech_gap: Current care home platforms (Nourish, Person Centred Software, Log my Care) capture daily observations and vital signs but do not analyse patterns or predict deterioration. Wearable and sensor-based monitoring (e.g., acoustic monitoring, motion sensors) exists but is typically standalone and not integrated with care planning software. Cera's predictive tool is designed for home care visits rather than residential care settings. Aston University's Smart Care Home project is in development. AI adds unique value by connecting disparate data streams (vitals, behaviour, nutrition, medication) and applying machine learning to identify deterioration patterns invisible to manual observation.
implementation_complexity: Requires integration and potentially process redesign. Full predictive systems need: sensor/wearable infrastructure (motion sensors, vital sign monitors), integration with existing care planning software, reliable Wi-Fi and data infrastructure, clinical governance frameworks for acting on AI alerts, and staff training on interpreting and responding to predictions. Simpler implementations (e.g., analysing existing digital care records for patterns) require less infrastructure. Aston University's Innovate UK-funded project is designed to work within existing care home environments.
proof_of_concept_availability: Yes — strong proof of concept. Cera's falls prediction tool (97% accuracy) is deployed at national scale across NHS care systems, processing 2 million+ visits monthly and reducing hospitalisations by up to 70%. Aston University's Smart Care Home project (with Lee Mount Healthcare, funded by Innovate UK) is building the UK's first integrated AI care home system. Government data shows acoustic monitoring reduces care home falls by 20% or more. Multiple published studies validate AI-based falls prediction in care settings.
